items.myth: If RSS goes up, you have a leak.
items.reality: RSS growth can be normal warm-up (loading models, building caches). A leak is *unbounded* growth over time at steady state. Always confirm with two snapshots after warm-up.
items.example: Service climbs from 100 MB to 2 GB on first 100 requests (caches), then stays flat at 2 GB — that's warm-up, not a leak.

items.myth: tracemalloc is too slow for production.
items.reality: It's not free, but it's far less invasive than memray. You can enable tracemalloc on a single canary pod or for a short window to diagnose a live leak.
items.example: Enable on one pod for 5 minutes; take two snapshots; disable. Investigation done.

items.reality: It only collects *unreachable* cycles; objects with live references (caches, queues, closures) are not reclaimed regardless of how many times you call it. Don't sprinkle `gc.collect()` as a band-aid — find the live reference.
items.example: An object held by an event-loop task is reachable; gc won't free it.

items.myth: Peak memory is the same as total allocated.
items.reality: Total = sum of every allocation over the run; peak = maximum live at one instant. For OOMs the peak matters; for fragmentation analysis the total matters. memray reports both — read the right one.
items.example: A function that allocates 50 GB total but frees as it goes may peak at only 500 MB.

items.myth: Switching from list to numpy is always cheaper.
items.reality: Numpy beats Python lists for *homogeneous numeric* data. For heterogeneous Python objects, numpy is worse (dtype=object reintroduces the per-object overhead).
items.example: 1M dicts → numpy(dtype=object) is no better than a Python list. Reach for an array library for numbers, not for arbitrary objects.
